metadata.title: The Limits of Interest: Moral economy and public engagement in the regulation of derivatives in the United States

abstract: This article analyzes the public comments submitted to the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC), 2010-2014, in response to proposed rules for implementing the Dodd-Frank reforms. By addressing a fine-grained typology of commenting organizations to a topic model of the combined comments, we illuminate a new pattern of public engagement in financial regulation. Contrary to the economic concept of regulatory capture, our data show no sharp divide between the suppliers of complex derivatives (the dealer banks) and their customers in other parts of the finance industry. In keeping with the general concept of financialization-but contrary to its strongest versions-our data show distinctly delimited evidence of a convergence between the banks and other sectors. Instead of either capture or all-embracing financialization, the comments submitted to the CFTC display an overriding opposition in the justifications used by commenters to explain their preferences. Commenters from the financial services and allied sectors consistently adopt a language of technical legitimation. Commenters in agriculture, livestock, retail energy, and many nonmarket organizations adopt a language of moral justification. The clarity of the divide between these two idioms indicates that the underlying purposes of new financial instruments are now being questioned. Even in the realm of esoteric financial instruments such as derivatives, the moral economy of market competition is once again subject to debate among market participants as well as observers.

paragraphs: The evolution of global capitalism has transformed financial-market regulation into one of the most contested yet opaque arenas of business-government relations. In no segment of finance is this juxtaposition more vivid than the derivatives market. As instruments whose value is based on movements in the prices of other assets, derivatives have always seemed complex if not entirely obscure to outside observers. Yet, since they played a central role in the financial crisis of 2007-2008, financial derivatives have also become the subject of exceptionally vehement debates in the politics of regulation.

The complexity of financial markets in capitalist societies was dramatically amplified by the extension of derivatives from the well-established use of options for agricultural commodities to the innovative world of purely financial options and swaps. Once the academic field of financial economics supplied models for quantifying the future value of interest rates and other revenue streams, the major banks started developing an multitude of so-called derivative contracts to fit the needs of particular customers (Johnson & Kwak, 2010, pp. 78-82). In the 1990s, the leading banks in the derivatives business began to hire risk management officers, who promptly turned risk management itself into a basis for profit centers that tended to diffuse new and more esoteric types of derivatives (Pernell et al., 2017). The spread of the derivatives trade from New York to major market countries around the world added yet another level of complexity (Quaglia, 2020).

After the financial crisis of 2008, the study of markets-which had previously focused on the efficiency of transactions-began to incorporate a greater concern for questions of basic fairness and the underlying moral foundations of market competition. New advocacy organizations, such as Americans for Financial Reform (AFR) and Better Markets, called for greater fairness in financial regulation. Scholarly analysts of capitalism called on their colleagues to pay more attention to moral categories and questions (Fourcade et al., 2013). Researchers viewed the major banks as the "culprits of the crisis" inasmuch as they used depositors' money in their own trading operations and then needed taxpayer bailouts, thereby perfecting the "privatization of profits and the socialization of losses" (Fligstein, 2021;Woll, 2013, p. 610). Other observers asked why no business leaders went to jail as they did after the Enron crisis of 2000-2001 and earlier financial scandals (see Eisinger, 2018;Rakoff, 2014). Still others questioned whether the increasingly technical nature of financial markets had undermined the regulatory process itself-suggesting the need for more democratic forms of financial regulation (Rahman, 2017).

This article analyzes the growing concern for the moral foundations of market competition, not only in the perspectives provided by scholars and analysts, but also in the everyday push and pull of regulatory politics. We show that this concern generated a consistent distinction-largely unnoticed in the recent literature on regulation-between two modes of argumentation that different groups use to advance their views. One major segment of commenters displays a much greater propensity to address regulators in a technically neutral idiom, framing their comments in technical language aimed at optimizing market transactions within a system they see as legitimate. By contrast, a different segment of commenters tends to address regulators in a morally evaluative idiom, emphasizing the need to ensure fair play and promote salutary market practices. While our data also show evidence for capture and financialization, this underappreciated distinction between technically neutral and morally evaluative idioms cuts across business and nonbusiness commenters in ways that are not predicted by current conceptual approaches.

We bring this growing concern to light by examining an original database of comments submitted to the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC)-the lead agency delegated by the Dodd-Frank Act of 2010 to construct a new framework for derivatives markets in the aftermath of the 2008 financial crisis. The Dodd-Frank Act, which was signed into law on July 21, 2010, enacted over 400 new rulemaking authorities to be implemented by various regulatory agencies. Under Title VII of the Act-which dealt with derivatives-the CFTC received roughly 60 of the new rule-making authorities to define prudential standards, document transactions, and supervise practices in the derivatives business. While the agency maintained ongoing enforcement activities for its prior regulations, the construction of the new regime for derivatives became the agency's central preoccupation during these years.

Our data include a complete set of the public comments received by the CFTC from 2010 to 2014 in response to the new rules it proposed to accomplish these regulatory tasks. Our analysis of this dataset aims to answer two core questions: (1) which specific issues are different commenters raising to the CFTC, and (2) how are they framing their arguments? While this database of comments provides the evidence for the article, our interpretation of the comments is also informed by 25 interviews conducted between 2013 and 2017. Interviewees included three or more representatives from the key regulatory agencies, elected legislators and their staffs, advocacy organizations, the major financial-service firms, their lobbyists, and lawyers.

Our approach centers on a topic-modeling analysis of the comments, which we structure according to a finegrained typology of the commenting organizations. This typology, discussed below, classifies commenting organizations according to their place in the "supply chain" of the derivatives business. To include all the relevant groups as well as the nonmarket observers who submitted comments, we divide commenters into 17 categories. After beginning with the key suppliers of derivatives (the dealer investment banks) and their customers (asset managers. mutual funds, and large firms), our categories become increasingly distant from the original suppliers. The categories further down the typology include associations of user firms, infrastructure providers, nonfinancial firms, and associations that make little use of derivatives but are affected by the way other firms use them. Finally, our typology also includes nonmarket observers such as legislators, academic experts, trade unions, consumer organizations, and others who took it upon themselves to offer recommendations to the CFTC. By arranging these commenting organizations in an encompassing but finely divided typology from suppliers to nonmarket observers, our approach provides a broader and more discriminating analysis than previous studies of the actors that tried to shape the CFTC's emerging regime for regulating derivatives.

Our analysis proceeds as follows. First, we review the literature on the politics of regulation, focusing on the concepts of capture, financialization, and moral economy. Next, we discuss the distinct place of derivatives in the politics of regulation. We then describe our original dataset, commenter typology, coding scheme, and implementation of Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) topic modeling. After describing our approach, we present our findings, organized around the two core questions of who comments on what and what different commentors are saying. In evaluating what commentors are saying, we present the 14 substantive topics derived from our dataset and explain how they illuminate the contrast between technically legitimating and morally justificatory language within and across commentor types. We then introduce the need for a moral contestation perspective to explain this divide. Finally, we conclude by reiterating the distinct nature of the divide between technically neutral and morally evaluative idioms and discussing its implications for regulatory politics.

sections.header_raw: Existing perspectives on the politics of regulation
sections.header_norm: existing perspectives on the politics of regulation
paragraphs: Our examination of the data is informed by a rich body of preexisting scholarly work on interest groups and regulatory politics. While parts of this literature also reflect the tension emphasized in this article between technical language and a moral perspective on financial markets, the moral implications of regulation have been far less developed or scrutinized in recent decades. Indeed, regulation is one of the spheres in which social scientists most assiduously elaborated upon the interest-driven strategies of market participants and their implications for efficiency versus rent-seeking rather than for fairness or moral consequences. Many of the most influential contributions on regulation revolved around the concepts of capture or financialization.

The idea that business interests could capture the regulatory agencies responsible for supervising them has its origins in an earlier literature on public administration. Political scientists including Huntington (1952) and Bernstein ([1955] 2015) expressed a concern in the 1950s that administrative agencies could lose their neutrality over time, while historians like Kolko ([1963] 1977) developed a view of business power in reassessing the progressive era (see Novak, 2014).

Abstracting from these views, Stigler (1971) developed a more formal economic perspective based on the collective action dynamics illuminated by Olson ([1965Olson ([ ] 1971)). Stigler asserted that the "profit maximizing" incentives in the oil supply industry, professional services, and rail haulage led incumbent firms to use regulation as a means of raising entry barriers against potential competitors (Stigler, 1971, p. 4). It was only rational for firms in these concentrated sectors, given their ample resources, to lobby regulators for elaborate safety and other rules that would raise the costs incurred by any new entrants trying to get a start. In derivatives regulation, the economic view implies that the large investment banks, as the product suppliers, should pursue a high-cost strategy, both to deter potential new suppliers and to gain oligopoly margins from everyone who used derivative instruments. This image of the key investment banks as a small set of incumbents who sought to preserve their oligopoly margins from greater competition was widely shared within the industry and explicitly confirmed by our background interviews with top regulators and industry insiders.

More recent scholarship shows that the economic theory of regulatory capture rests on only one of several mechanisms by which businesses try to shape rulemaking (Carpenter & Moss, 2014). Instead of raising costs that represent entry barriers to new firms, incumbent firms can dilute regulatory standards by pushing regulators toward "less stringent or less costly" measures than those which maximize the public interest, implying a political strategy that Dan Carpenter calls "corrosive capture" (Carpenter, 2014). By supplying information, helpful language, and personnel to regulatory agencies, incumbent firms can reinforce the strategy of corrosive capture through a less visible mechanism of "cultural capture" (Kwak, 2014). One condition that frequently facilitates cultural capture is the low salience that allows incumbent firms to shape regulations through the methods of what Pepper Culpepper calls "quiet politics" (Culpepper, 2010). These newer mechanisms of capture can, however, be counterbalanced when the proponents of regulation make their case more salient through consumer alliances (Trumbull, 2012) and organized public pressure campaigns (Judge-Lord, 2019).

In addition to the multiplicity of capture mechanisms that scholars have documented in the last decade, the overarching process of financialization has undermined the sharp boundaries between supplier firms and customers that underpinned the narrow economic concept of capture. According to the financialization concept as elaborated by Krippner (2011) and van der Zwan (2014), firms in many sectors have learned to extract a growing part of their revenues from financial transactions such as consumer lending, corporate real estate operations, subscription services, and portfolio investment by corporate treasurers.

The use of derivatives is one of the quintessential examples of financialization. Derivatives enable firms to transform the supply and cost risks in product markets such as cars or pharmaceuticals into derivative assets, which can then be traded in financial markets. The spread of derivatives and similar financial instruments has effectively detached companies from their sectoral roots, allowing all firms to operate like financial firms. In the words of Steve Galbraith, a Morgan Stanley investment strategist in 2002, "Corporate America is rapidly becoming Bank America" (quoted in Ip, 2002;repeated in Krippner, 2011, p. 4).

Given the evolving map of interests created by multiple capture mechanisms and the overarching process of financialization, we should expect to see the largest incumbent firms-which, in derivatives, means the core investment banks-pursuing their interests in regulatory affairs through a range of coalition-building strategies. Yackee and coauthors show that firms have refined their strategies for influencing regulations (Yackee & Yackee, 2006), including through highly pragmatic choices about when to expand their lobbying coalition by co-signing their comments with firms in allied business areas (Nelson & Yackee, 2012). Similarly, in financial regulation, Pagliari and Young analyze interest constellations across numerous U.S., European, and international cases and find that the financial-services firms conveyed priorities that were substantially shared by firms in telecommunications, health, energy, and agriculture-a pattern they refer to as "leveraged interests" (Pagliari & Young, 2014, 2016). For derivatives in particular, Pagliari shows how financial firms and their allies were, despite growing diversity in the range of engaged actors, able to dilute the stringency of the new regulations (Pagliari, 2018).

These studies illuminate key dynamics in the politics of regulation by showing how the interests of incumbent firms extend significantly more widely across business sectors than predicted by the classic economic theory of regulation posited by Stigler (1971). Yet, while they document important commonalities of interest in postenactment regulatory politics, they do not explain where these shared interests end, or why. Has the process of financialization become so pervasive that it blurs the distinctions between suppliers and users and creates an entirely new set of monolithic business preferences in the use of derivatives? Or, alternatively, is the heterogeneity of business interests so pronounced that there is no pattern beyond the rule-specific calculations of particular firms to explain when incumbent competitors form regulatory coalitions? Or do some parts of the business world remain completely immune to finance-driven practices for reasons that fall into new and unforeseen patterns? Our research provides answers to these questions by analyzing the contours and cleavages among the comments submitted by all organizations-both within and beyond the financial sector-that had views on the CFTC's proposed rules for the emerging regulatory framework for derivatives.

While our data are largely compatible with the revised mechanisms of regulatory capture and expanded coalition-building documented in recent literature, the central distinction they highlight between technically neutral and morally evaluative modes of discourse has, we argue, been insufficiently examined. This distinction is the clearest axis that runs consistently through the entire body of comments. Since our data do not extend back before 2010, we do not know if this contrast characterized the notice-and-comment process in earlier debates over financial-market regulation. It is, however, clear that the moral critique of regulatory policy received surprisingly little attention in the scholarly literature on financial market regulation during the decades of neoliberal growth from the 1970s into the early 2000s. During these decades, when the advanced economies adopted competition as the leading principle of what Fred Block and Margaret Somers call "market fundamentalism," the discussion of a moral foundation for regulatory policy was largely pushed to the sidelines of established policy debate (Block & Somers, 2014).

The cleavage in our database between a technical versus a moral framing instead harkens back to an earlier perspective that looks beyond the interplay of material interests and focuses on the moral dimension of markets as institutions that support or undermine social cohesion. This earlier perspective was forcefully asserted in the early 1900s by Louis Brandeis, the Progressive lawyer and later Supreme Court justice, who elaborated a concept of "regulated competition" that set business at the service of larger social goals (Berk, 2009). A parallel strand of thinking on the moral underpinnings and consequences of capitalism is traced by Rogan (2017) through the 20th-century British historians R. H. Tawney, Karl Polanyi, and E. P. Thompson. In particular, E. P. Thompson's work on late 18th-century grain trading practices in England reveals a pattern of moral protest with which our findings show some unmistakable parallels.

Except for such voices in historical studies and social theory, the moral economy perspective was-as Carugati and Levi (2021) make clear-largely absent during the decades of neoliberal ascendancy. Only after the financial crisis of 2008 have scholars such as Sabeel Rahman (2017) addressed a morally evaluative perspective to financial markets by calling for a re-anchoring of regulatory goals in the foundations of democratic theory. In the literature on financial regulation, Orban (2013) provided one of the few empirical studies that focuses on moral argumentation as a key part of the debate that preceded the Dodd-Frank Act. We argue in this article that moral argumentation has come to occupy an even larger place in the less publicized politics that shaped the postenactment implementation of the Dodd-Frank statutes for derivatives after the law's passage in 2010.

In addition to the multiplicity of capture mechanisms that scholars have documented in the last decade, the overarching process of financialization has undermined the sharp boundaries between supplier firms and customers that underpinned the narrow economic concept of capture. According to the financialization concept as elaborated by Krippner (2011) and van der Zwan (2014), firms in many sectors have learned to extract a growing part of their revenues from financial transactions such as consumer lending, corporate real estate operations, subscription services, and portfolio investment by corporate treasurers.

The use of derivatives is one of the quintessential examples of financialization. Derivatives enable firms to transform the supply and cost risks in product markets such as cars or pharmaceuticals into derivative assets, which can then be traded in financial markets. The spread of derivatives and similar financial instruments has effectively detached companies from their sectoral roots, allowing all firms to operate like financial firms. In the words of Steve Galbraith, a Morgan Stanley investment strategist in 2002, "Corporate America is rapidly becoming Bank America" (quoted in Ip, 2002;repeated in Krippner, 2011, p. 4).

Given the evolving map of interests created by multiple capture mechanisms and the overarching process of financialization, we should expect to see the largest incumbent firms-which, in derivatives, means the core investment banks-pursuing their interests in regulatory affairs through a range of coalition-building strategies. Yackee and coauthors show that firms have refined their strategies for influencing regulations (Yackee & Yackee, 2006), including through highly pragmatic choices about when to expand their lobbying coalition by co-signing their comments with firms in allied business areas (Nelson & Yackee, 2012). Similarly, in financial regulation, Pagliari and Young analyze interest constellations across numerous U.S., European, and international cases and find that the financial-services firms conveyed priorities that were substantially shared by firms in telecommunications, health, energy, and agriculture-a pattern they refer to as "leveraged interests" (Pagliari & Young, 2014, 2016). For derivatives in particular, Pagliari shows how financial firms and their allies were, despite growing diversity in the range of engaged actors, able to dilute the stringency of the new regulations (Pagliari, 2018).

These studies illuminate key dynamics in the politics of regulation by showing how the interests of incumbent firms extend significantly more widely across business sectors than predicted by the classic economic theory of regulation posited by Stigler (1971). Yet, while they document important commonalities of interest in postenactment regulatory politics, they do not explain where these shared interests end, or why. Has the process of financialization become so pervasive that it blurs the distinctions between suppliers and users and creates an entirely new set of monolithic business preferences in the use of derivatives? Or, alternatively, is the heterogeneity of business interests so pronounced that there is no pattern beyond the rule-specific calculations of particular firms to explain when incumbent competitors form regulatory coalitions? Or do some parts of the business world remain completely immune to finance-driven practices for reasons that fall into new and unforeseen patterns? Our research provides answers to these questions by analyzing the contours and cleavages among the comments submitted by all organizations-both within and beyond the financial sector-that had views on the CFTC's proposed rules for the emerging regulatory framework for derivatives.

While our data are largely compatible with the revised mechanisms of regulatory capture and expanded coalition-building documented in recent literature, the central distinction they highlight between technically neutral and morally evaluative modes of discourse has, we argue, been insufficiently examined. This distinction is the clearest axis that runs consistently through the entire body of comments. Since our data do not extend back before 2010, we do not know if this contrast characterized the notice-and-comment process in earlier debates over financial-market regulation. It is, however, clear that the moral critique of regulatory policy received surprisingly little attention in the scholarly literature on financial market regulation during the decades of neoliberal growth from the 1970s into the early 2000s. During these decades, when the advanced economies adopted competition as the leading principle of what Fred Block and Margaret Somers call "market fundamentalism," the discussion of a moral foundation for regulatory policy was largely pushed to the sidelines of established policy debate (Block & Somers, 2014).

The cleavage in our database between a technical versus a moral framing instead harkens back to an earlier perspective that looks beyond the interplay of material interests and focuses on the moral dimension of markets as institutions that support or undermine social cohesion. This earlier perspective was forcefully asserted in the early 1900s by Louis Brandeis, the Progressive lawyer and later Supreme Court justice, who elaborated a concept of "regulated competition" that set business at the service of larger social goals (Berk, 2009). A parallel strand of thinking on the moral underpinnings and consequences of capitalism is traced by Rogan (2017) through the 20th-century British historians R. H. Tawney, Karl Polanyi, and E. P. Thompson. In particular, E. P. Thompson's work on late 18th-century grain trading practices in England reveals a pattern of moral protest with which our findings show some unmistakable parallels.

Except for such voices in historical studies and social theory, the moral economy perspective was-as Carugati and Levi (2021) make clear-largely absent during the decades of neoliberal ascendancy. Only after the financial crisis of 2008 have scholars such as Sabeel Rahman (2017) addressed a morally evaluative perspective to financial markets by calling for a re-anchoring of regulatory goals in the foundations of democratic theory. In the literature on financial regulation, Orban (2013) provided one of the few empirical studies that focuses on moral argumentation as a key part of the debate that preceded the Dodd-Frank Act. We argue in this article that moral argumentation has come to occupy an even larger place in the less publicized politics that shaped the postenactment implementation of the Dodd-Frank statutes for derivatives after the law's passage in 2010.

Our examination of the data is informed by a rich body of preexisting scholarly work on interest groups and regulatory politics. While parts of this literature also reflect the tension emphasized in this article between technical language and a moral perspective on financial markets, the moral implications of regulation have been far less developed or scrutinized in recent decades. Indeed, regulation is one of the spheres in which social scientists most assiduously elaborated upon the interest-driven strategies of market participants and their implications for efficiency versus rent-seeking rather than for fairness or moral consequences. Many of the most influential contributions on regulation revolved around the concepts of capture or financialization.

The idea that business interests could capture the regulatory agencies responsible for supervising them has its origins in an earlier literature on public administration. Political scientists including Huntington (1952) and Bernstein ([1955] 2015) expressed a concern in the 1950s that administrative agencies could lose their neutrality over time, while historians like Kolko ([1963] 1977) developed a view of business power in reassessing the progressive era (see Novak, 2014).

sections.header_raw: Derivatives and the politics of regulation
sections.header_norm: derivatives and the politics of regulation
paragraphs: Derivatives occupy a key place in the literature on regulation in substantive as well as theoretical terms. According to the most comprehensive study to date, the derivatives trade had become the world's largest market by the early 2000s (Spagna, 2018). Most observers saw the growth of financial derivatives as an essential factor in causing the financial crisis of 2008 (Helleiner et al., 2018;The Financial Crisis Inquiry Commission, 2011, p. xiv, 45ff;Ziegler & Woolley, 2016). While derivatives had been used for centuries as options to purchase or sell agricultural products at a particular price in the future, purely financial derivatives appeared in the 1980s. These financial derivatives first took the form of options to swap one interest rate or currency for another, and later as ever more complex contracts based on the future price of any number of underlying assets.

The increasingly esoteric nature of financial derivatives means that the task of regulating them offers a theoretically revealing case for multiple perspectives. According to choice-theoretic approaches, financial derivatives create the kind of information asymmetries that leave regulatory agencies dependent on market participants for learning about evolving products, risks, and competitive conditions (Gailmard & Patty, 2012;McCarty, 2017). Other scholars see derivatives as legally constructed assets anchored in conventions that benefit privileged groups (Pistor, 2019;Wigan, 2009). Still others see financial derivatives as an example of innovation that requires new and more flexible approaches that can align market regulations with deeper societal goals and aspirations (Ford, 2017).

In this article, we ask how different market participants and observers understood the regulatory task faced by the CFTC as it built a new regime for regulating derivatives in the wake of the 2008 financial crisis. For this purpose, a topic-modeling approach that analyzes the language used across different groups of industry and nonindustry actors is particularly appropriate. We assemble the comments submitted by these actors into a single corpus and use LDA topic modeling to distill the key topics raised by these comments. LDA allows us to see whether the topics prioritized by different commenter groups in our typology align with patterns from existing theories of regulatory politics-or whether other patterns appear more prominently. We describe the data produced by the public comment process as well as the specific modeling approach that we use to construct our topics in the following sections. We then explain our typology of industry and nonindustry actors, analyze the contours of their comments, and explain how the overall pattern of public engagement reveals an alternative theoretical perspective based on the divide between the idioms of technical legitimation and moral evaluation.

The CFTC data include metadata about each comment, notably the organization which the commenter indicated they were affiliated with. Unfortunately, organization was an optional field in the comment form, leading to a subset of 8262 comments available for analysis. Some of these letters had multiple types of signatories listed in organization (161 with two, 27 with three, one with four) which we chose to duplicate for each relevant commenter type. Unless otherwise specified, all references to "the corpus" from here onward means the group of 8451 comments (including multi-signatories) with an interpretable organization value that we used to classify each comment into our typology of commenter types. This corpus forms the basis of our substantive analysis. 2

paragraphs: We developed a 17-part typology which classifies the unique organization values into theoretically distinct types of commenters. The typology appears in Table 1 below. Our categories are ordered from the narrowest definition of the regulated industry (1) to the groups explicitly representing citizens' concerns more generally (17). Since the Wall Street banks had become the major sell-side derivatives dealers, category 1 includes the eight banks that consistently ranked as dominant derivatives dealers. Less dominant banks make up category 2. The six most central financial services associations make up category 3. All other financial associations are included in the open-ended category 4. Categories 5 through 11 consist of other business segments that move incrementally away from core "suppliers" of financial services to users of financial services such as asset managers (5 and 6), the U.S. Chamber of Commerce and affiliates (7), transaction managers and infrastructure firms (8), advisory, consultancy and law firms (9), private-sector associations from nonfinancial sectors (10), and individual firms outside financial services (11).

Categories 12 through 17 include commenters outside the realm of for-profit market participants. They include government officials ( 12), university and other experts ( 13), consumer advocacy and other public-interest groups ( 14), trade unions and other employee associations (15), pro-market advocacy groups ( 16), and unaffiliated individuals (17). The last category captures a small number of commenters whose organization value could not be positively identified (18).

Using our typology, we had two coders separately code all 4671 programmatically unique comments. 3 Coders dealt only with organization values and did not have access to the individual comment content or other metadata to ensure that all organizations were classified solely on the information contained in the organization value Discrepancies between the two coders were reconciled during a meeting with the authors after both coders finished an isolated initial pass through the data. The reconciled values were then reviewed by the authors to confirm the correct classification of well-known organizations as well as the organizations commenting explicitly on behalf of other categories. 4

sections.paper_id: Ziegler et al. - 2024 - The Limits of Interest Moral economy and public engagement in the regulation of derivatives in the
paragraphs: As our research question seeks to map the primary concerns of commenting organizations, we employed LDA topic modeling to generate topics which we then analyzed for patterns using our typology of commenter categories. Other scholars have fruitfully employed methods such as substring matching (Judge-Lord, 2019; Pagliari & Young, 2020) to show how commenting organizations build coalitions. Since our interest lies in illuminating general themes that characterize all commenting organizations, we use a topic modeling approach, following the advice of Junk who recommends topic modeling as well suited to understanding the "camps" or "sides" of an issue (Junk, 2019, p. 660;cited in Pagliari & Young, 2020, p. 1703). Compared to structured topic models, LDA is more strictly inductive, since it requires no prior specification of correlates. Its inherent naiveté produces a degree of noisiness in its topics, which necessitates somewhat more human interpretation than other approaches. At the same time, LDA is particularly well-suited to the goal of discovery that Grimmer et al. (2022) emphasize. Ziegler et al. - 2024 - The Limits of Interest Moral economy and public engagement in the regulation of derivatives in the
paragraphs: While the topic model provides the basis of our analysis, the distribution of commenters among rules validates the specificity and descriptiveness of our typology. Here, we demonstrate our typology's breadth (to capture significant variation in commenting patterns) and granularity (to make meaningful distinctions between commenter issue positions). We also validate a core LDA assumption-that our corpus represents one large conversationby showing that commenter types contribute in some degree across all topics and rules rather than limiting themselves to isolated pockets of topics or rules. 6 Industry and nonindustry organizations commented in roughly equal proportions, countering the economic capture hypothesis while confirming our typology's appropriate granularity. Industry actors submitted 4482 of 8351 comments (53.7%) received by the CFTC, while nonindustry commenters accounted for 3869 of 8351 (46.3%). 7 These balanced proportions contradict the key expectation from capture theory that the central industry insiders-sell-side commenters (1 through 4)-should overwhelmingly shape the rules.

According to the classic or "Stiglerian" concept of economic capture, the incumbent banks should particularly want to dominate the conversation around derivatives, since the CFTC's proposed rules on these instruments would constrain one of their highest-margin businesses. Yet, the Major Wall Street Sell-Side Banks (1) and the Other Sell-Side Banks (2) together accounted for only 3.4% (283/8351) of comments submitted. If we suppose large banks might submit comments through trade associations, we can add Core Financial Service Trade Associations (3) and Other Financial Sector Associations (4)-which still accounts for only 13.6% (1137/8351) of the comments.

The limits of the capture concept are also illuminated by a simple list of the most frequently commenting organizations. Table 2 shows that none of the major investment banks were among the 10 most frequent commenters. The financial services were more generally represented by the asset managers (e.g., Black Rock) and their trade associations (the MFA and the Asset Management Group in SIFMA) and the industry-wide associations (ISDA and the Financial Services Roundtable) that included both sell-side banks and their customers. Especially notable were the advocacy groups-AFR and Better Markets, Inc.-as the first and third most frequently commenting organizations in the database. Both organizations were founded expressly to press the regulatory agencies to adopt stringent rules for implementing the Dodd-Frank legislation. Through their expertise and staying power, these two organizations submitted comments to the CFTC on almost all of the new rules for derivatives, thereby contesting the industry actors that would otherwise have dominated the process. Even more than the simple numbers of comments, our topic model of the entire corpus illuminates the contours of the public comments. Since we combined all comments submitted to the CFTC in response to Dodd-Frank into a single corpus, the substantive topics extracted by LDA represent a statistically neutral profile of the entire discussion around the CFTC's rules. The number of topics, which we set at 14, is a choice that investigators must make in order to generate the most coherent lists of terms (see Chang et al., 2009;Grimmer et al., 2022, pp. 6-7). It is unsurprising that the topics reflect those proposed rules that generated the largest number of comments. Even within this general tendency, however, significant differences are apparent. Of the 14 topics generated by our LDA analysis, 11 fall into coherent family groups while the other three represent less coherent standalone issues The first four topics in Table 3 revolve around specific rules authorized by Dodd-Frank. Topic I focuses on the proposed rule for regulating cross-border swaps when one party was geographically based in another country. Topic II revolves around the Volcker Rule, which prohibited proprietary trading by banks that made use of federal guarantees. Topic III concerns the need to accommodate longstanding users of derivatives, such as airlines or electricity producers, who use them to hedge core business risk. Topic IV revolves around two specific compliance rules that imposed procedures to safeguard whistleblowers and required major swap market participants to hire chief compliance officers.

The next two topics (V and VI) concern transformative steps in the CFTC's new architecture for regulating derivatives: swaps clearing and swaps reporting. These steps were part of a logical sequence aimed at creating visible prices: registering all significant participants in the derivatives markets, including hedge funds; reporting all derivatives transactions to the CFTC (not merely an industry data depository); clearing all noncustomized swaps through a licensed clearing organization that guarantees both sides of the contract; and, eventually, executing the trades through an open facility to allow public price discovery. These steps promised to transform the derivatives business from a closed club of major swaps dealers to an open marketplace. Of the four steps, the requirements for clearing and reporting swaps generated the largest numbers of comments as reflected in Topics V and VI. The requirements for registering market participants and for executing trades through registered "swap execution facilities" (SEFs) prompted fewer comments across these and other topics.

The next three topics (VII, VIII, and IX) display the composite nature of topics that can be produced by LDA. Topic VII reflects comments that invoke macro-level or systemic issues. These include individual comments on risk levels at the large banks, extended discussions of liquidity submitted by smaller independent firms, and lengthy letters from academic economists comparing the Volcker Rule to capital adequacy rules as alternative approaches to macro-level stability.

A more mundane composite is exemplified by Topic VIII, composed of comments with long lists of signatories and addresses. Because LDA generates topics by looking for groups of words used together, these lists were combined because they included extensive city and state information. Topic IX is another composite based on otherwise disparate comments that mention firm-level risk from various contexts. The connecting thread concerns the unexpected losses incurred in J.P. Morgan's London branch, April-May, 2012. Along with reaction to this "London Whale" trading scandal, this topic includes a lengthy letter from U.S. Senator Carl Levin (Posch et al., 2019, Control Number IDs 59278 and 59287) along with a number of idiosyncratic comments submitted by unaffiliated individuals.

The next two topics (X and XI) return to a coherent focus on particular categories of derivatives users. Several types of firms sought exemptions from certain rules because their business models depended on derivatives to purchase inputs and assure prices in advance. These self-described "end users" included airlines, farmers, electricity suppliers and other utilities, as well as banks and multinational firms that used foreign exchange swaps to hedge against currency fluctuations. Topic X reflects the contested nature of the CFTC's end-user definition by combining comments against exempting the large banks as end users. Topic XI gathers electricity cooperatives, private power suppliers, pipeline operators, and other utilities that all argued they should-as nonfinancial end users-be exempted from the CFTC's proposed clearing and margin rules.

The final three topics (XII, XIII, and XIV) relate to the proposed limits on the maximum position any firm or dealer could accumulate in particular commodity markets. Many of these comments explicitly weighed speculative trading versus "bona fide" uses of commodity swaps. Interestingly, all three topics included key terms-"speculation" and "manipulation"-suggesting that longstanding physical commodity users and producers saw their markets as vulnerable to deep-pocketed newcomers. Two of these topics revolve around precious metals or agricultural products while the third emphasizes basic households needs for foodstuffs, gas, and oil.

sections.header_raw: Who cares most about which issues?
sections.header_norm: who cares most about which issues
paragraphs: The heart of our analysis shows whether commenters display distinct concerns by concentrating their attention on certain topics according to their place in our typology. LDA allows us to explore commenters' priorities by calculating the probability that a typical comment from each commenter group will draw from each of the 14 topics. These type-specific values-known as topic proportions-tell us which topics matter most to the average commenter in a particular category. By comparing whether, for example, the major investment banks (1) address the same issues as the private asset managers (6), the advisory firms (9), the nonfinancial firms (11), or nonmarket organizations (12-16), we can discern how far investment banks' interests extend into the financial-services and nonfinancial business worlds.

Based on our typology (Table 1), the similarities and differences in these topic proportions speak directly to different views of regulation. According to the strong or Stiglerian concept of economic capture, the major Wall Street dealer banks should, as the incumbent suppliers, have displayed have displayed distinct priorities regarding new regulations. Alternatively, insofar as the dealer banks' priorities are shared by broader financial-services associations or by firms in the "downstream" user sectors, the evidence would provide support for the concept of "leveraged interest" developed by Pagliari and Young (2014) as well as the commonality of interest implied by the broader financialization hypothesis. Accordingly, we analyze the topic proportions by noting which topics mattered most to the Major Wall Street sell-side banks and comparing these priorities to those of commenters in downstream sectors, and then to the different types of nonmarket actors organizations that participated in the notice-and-comment process.

Our analysis reveals an overall pattern of disparate priorities that show considerable overlap in the concerns expressed by different types of private-sector organizations, but only up to a point. Within the financial services themselves, major derivatives users in asset management (commenter group 6) align roughly with the banks on only half or our topics. Meanwhile, the financial infrastructure firms (commenter group 8) agree with the dealer banks about which topics are unimportant while diverging markedly from the them on which topics are important. More consistent with the concept of extended interest and other perspectives on financialization, the large manufacturing firms that mobilized through the U.S. Chamber of Commerce and its affiliates (commenter group 7) do indeed emphasize many of the same topics as the large Wall Street banks. However, these concerns do not extend to the smaller firms and cooperatives classified in nonfinancial sector associations (10) and nonfinancial firms (11). Much more consistent than any of these patchwork commonalities across comment groups 1 through 9 is the pervasive tendency to rely on technical arguments in comments to the CFTC, though this tendency also stops very clearly at the nonfinancial associations (10) and firms (11).

However, these concerns do not extend to the smaller firms and cooperatives classified in nonfinancial sector associations (10) and nonfinancial firms (11). Much more consistent than any of these patchwork commonalities across comment groups 1 through 9 is the pervasive tendency to rely on technical arguments in comments to the CFTC, though this tendency also stops very clearly at the nonfinancial associations (10) and firms (11).
sections.text_with_footnotes:
The heart of our analysis shows whether commenters display distinct concerns by concentrating their attention on certain topics according to their place in our typology. LDA allows us to explore commenters' priorities by calculating the probability that a typical comment from each commenter group will draw from each of the 14 topics. These type-specific values-known as topic proportions-tell us which topics matter most to the average commenter in a particular category. By comparing whether, for example, the major investment banks (1) address the same issues as the private asset managers (6), the advisory firms (9), the nonfinancial firms (11), or nonmarket organizations (12-16), we can discern how far investment banks' interests extend into the financial-services and nonfinancial business worlds.

Based on our typology (Table 1), the similarities and differences in these topic proportions speak directly to different views of regulation. According to the strong or Stiglerian concept of economic capture, the major Wall Street dealer banks should, as the incumbent suppliers, have displayed have displayed distinct priorities regarding new regulations. Alternatively, insofar as the dealer banks' priorities are shared by broader financial-services associations or by firms in the "downstream" user sectors, the evidence would provide support for the concept of "leveraged interest" developed by Pagliari and Young (2014) as well as the commonality of interest implied by the broader financialization hypothesis. Accordingly, we analyze the topic proportions by noting which topics mattered most to the Major Wall Street sell-side banks and comparing these priorities to those of commenters in downstream sectors, and then to the different types of nonmarket actors organizations that participated in the notice-and-comment process.

Our analysis reveals an overall pattern of disparate priorities that show considerable overlap in the concerns expressed by different types of private-sector organizations, but only up to a point. Within the financial services themselves, major derivatives users in asset management (commenter group 6) align roughly with the banks on only half or our topics. Meanwhile, the financial infrastructure firms (commenter group 8) agree with the dealer banks about which topics are unimportant while diverging markedly from the them on which topics are important. sections.header_raw: The prevalence of technical vocabulary among the banks and their key customers
sections.header_norm: the prevalence of technical vocabulary among the banks and their key customers
paragraphs: As Figure 1 shows, the major supply side banks (1) overwhelmingly prioritized two topics: cross-border transactions (I) and the Volcker Rule (II). Together, these two topics accounted for over 45% of the aggregate attention that the major supply-side banks directed to the CFTC's Dodd-Frank rulemakings. The only other topic that merited more than 10% of the banks' overall attention pertained to the swaps clearing rule (V). Of the remaining topics, four account for 5%-10%, while seven show negligible proportions of concern.

Unsurprisingly, the other sell-side banks (2) have priorities closely congruent with the eight major sell-side banks. They share an intense concern for cross-border transactions as well as the Volcker Rule, and diverge only slightly on the other topics.

The next commenter group (3) consists of the seven major financial-service trade associations active in Washington policy debates. While some industry insiders say these associations are dominated by the major dealer banks, their priorities should reflect a broader swath of the financial services firms. We therefore separate the seven "core" associations from other financial sector associations (4). These core associations parallel the major sell-side banks only roughly, putting higher priority on the rules for compliance (IV) and reporting (VI) without showing much concern for cross-border transactions (I) or the Volcker Rule (II). The other financial associations (4) display slightly less congruence with the major banks, showing a similar level of concern for only half the topics and, like the core associations, high concern for compliance (IV) and swap reporting (VI). Since these results display neither the clear distinctions nor commonalities of interest that would align with either regulatory capture by the dominant banks or with a broader financialization pattern, broader shared interest, the next group of commenters-asset management firms-is particularly important to our analysis.

Asset managers-mutual fund companies, sovereign wealth funds, and worldwide portfolio managers that invest capital for institutions, governments, and wealthy individuals-use derivatives to hedge foreign currencies, smooth interest rates, insure low-quality bonds, and construct exchange traded funds that mimic popular indexes (Braun, 2022;Thatcher & Vlandas, 2022). Since the public asset managers (5) submitted fewer than 30 of the 8351 comments, we focus only on their private-sector counterparts (6). 8 Including wealth management firms like Blackrock, Fidelity, and Citadel, the private asset managers are major buy-side participants in swap markets. Given their buy-side position, they put higher priority than do the Wall Street banks on the end-user rule (IV). Like the Wall Street banks, they are moderately focused on the swaps clearing rule (V), but otherwise parallel the banks only in low levels of concern for swaps reporting (VI), end users and public utilities (XI), and agricultural commodities (XIII). Most notably, private asset managers do not share the banks' pronounced concern for cross-border swaps (I) or the Volcker Rule (II). As major consumers of financial derivatives, these firms should-according to economic capture-favor changes that open the supply side of the derivatives market. While particular firms such as Citadel saw the CFTC as loosening the banks' grip on derivatives, the topic proportions in Figure 1 show a pattern of varying concern across the financial services that does not discriminate clearly among our hypotheses. Indeed, private asset managers largely ignored the issues of highest concern to investment banks, diverged sharply from the banks on a few issues, and shared a low proportion on topics of minimal interest to both groups.

We give the U.S. Chamber of Commerce a separate place in our typology (7) because it spans financial services as well as manufacturing. The Chamber contributed conspicuously to the legislative and postenactment debates regarding Dodd-Frank, establishing two affiliates expressly to lobby the federal government. One, the Center for Capital Competitiveness, argued for strong financial services and deep capital markets. The second, the Coalition of Derivatives End Users (CDEU), took shape in the legislative phase when Cummins Engine, IBM, and Cargill came together with several sectoral associations. The Chamber and its affiliate organizations overlapped with the banks almost as often as the private asset managers and it shared the banks' emphatic interest in the Volcker Rule (II). The Chamber's commenting priorities therefore represent unambiguous evidence for the downstream extension of the banks' priorities that Pagliari and Young (2014) describe as "leveraged interest." This pattern also reflects the broader financialization hypothesis that predicts more commonality between the major banks and their industrial customers than would be expected from the economic capture hypothesis.

This pattern of shared priorities does not, however, extend to all firms. Even within financial services, market infrastructure firms (8) that provide independent trading platforms, clearing houses, software and technology for the newly required market facilities display a distinct set of commenting concerns. Their topic proportions initially appear similar to those of the U.S. Chamber, approximating the priorities of the major sell-side banks on roughly half the topics. Yet infrastructure firms focus more than 50% of their commenting activity on two topics of limited interest to the banks-the proposed rules for clearing (V) and swaps reporting (VI). Meanwhile, they display negligible attention to the topics of intensive concern to the banks-cross-border transactions (I) and the Volcker Rule (II). Infrastructure providers appear to be confirmed supporters of new rules that impose broader market transparency on previously closed transactions.

The banks' priorities are even less pronounced among nonfinancial firms (11) and associations (10). Together, these categories include most nonfinancial end users whose businesses required derivatives for fuel, energy, agricultural commodities, silver, and other metals. Comprised of energy delivery companies, rural power suppliers, and agricultural cooperatives, the nonfinancial associations showed most concern for the end-user rule (III), especially as it concerned public utilities (XI). The nonfinancial firms showed exceptional engagement in the discussion of small banks and end-users (X), precious metals (XI), and household commodities (XIV). Neither category showed significant similarities to the banks on more than two topics.

These results provide little evidence for the classic Stiglerian theory of economic capture and strong but sharply demarcated evidence of the shared regulatory interests implied by the financialization concept. Accordingly, despite some overlap with more recent mechanisms of corrosive and cultural capture and clear compatibility with concept of leveraged interest, our analysis shows, at most, qualified confirmation for either of the two primary interest-driven perspectives in the literature.

sections.paper_id: Ziegler et al. - 2024 - The Limits of Interest Moral economy and public engagement in the regulation of derivatives in the
sections.header_raw: The appearance of moral evaluation in the comments
sections.header_norm: the appearance of moral evaluation in the comments
paragraphs: If we move further down our typology, an entirely different pattern comes into view. The nonfinancial firms along with the commenter groups outside industry focused on a set of topics that revealed an expansive body of morally framed viewpoints in the debate over a new regulatory regime for derivatives. Moral argumentation was not the only vocabulary that these groups used. The advocacy organizations such as AFR and Better Markets regularly mobilized deep expertise from their internal staff and external specialists. In addition to the waves of group comments they sometimes prompted, they wrote their own well-informed letters whose importance was acknowledged in interviews with top legislators and regulators. Similarly, agricultural producers and suppliers knew their markets intimately and often made interest-based arguments around supply, demand, liquidity, and volatility in commodity markets. As the examples quoted below from the Kansas Board of Trade and the American Bakers Association (ABA) show, however, these organizations framed their arguments as moral statements regarding the stability and fairness of commodity markets that were expected to serve the needs of American households as well as producers.

More specifically, as illuminated in Figure 1, the nonindustry categories (12-17) in our typology focused on bank eligibility for the end-user exception (X) and the three topics that distinguish between speculative versus bona fide trading activity in commodities (XII, XIII, and XIV). Government commenters ( 12), international as well as subnational, emphasized cross-border trading (I), followed by compliance rules (IV) and the definition of end-user status (X). Academic specialists (13), advocacy groups ( 14), labor unions (15) coincide with nonmarket firms (11) in focusing on the boundaries of the "end-user" designation (X). The same three nonmarket categories join with both nonfinancial associations (10) and nonfinancial firms (11) in commenting on position limits and market manipulation in precious metals (XII), agricultural and energy products (XIII), and household commodities (XIV). Nonmarket commenters and-even more notably the nonfinancial firms-were especially concerned with food and household necessities (XIV). The different topics (X, XII, XIII, and XIV) that gather evaluative terms like speculation and manipulation delineate a cleavage that appears with increasing clarity toward the lower end of our commenter typology on topics that address the moral dimension of unrestrained derivatives trading.

Variations in composition among the four topics are important because they show that the contrast between technical and moral language cuts across the business versus nonbusiness divide. Market actors in agriculture, energy supply, and retail goods, often from rural states, conspicuously prioritize morally freighted language in many of their comments. At the same time, while many of their comment letters address the CFTC's proposed rules on position limits, they used similar language in comments on the end-user exemption and the definition of allowable or "bona fide" hedging that would be exempt from CFTC rules for documenting and clearing swap transactions.

To illuminate the dominant place of such moral claims in one of the two major parts of our corpus, it is well worth looking further into their substance. The use of moral language was far from simple or univocal. Different groups put moral argumentation to quite distinct uses in their comments on the Dodd-Frank rules.

Simple indignation was the primary theme in several waves of comment letters prompted by advocacy organizations such as AFR and the U.S. Public Interest Research Group. One such set, from small business owners of varied types, asked the CFTC to withhold end-user exemptions from purely financial organizations that "want to gamble in derivatives markets" in ways that "would throw important trades back into the shadows" (e.g., Rushland Landscapers, February 18, 2011, archived in Posch et al., 2019, Control Number ID 35253). Numerous oil and gas retailers such as Paxson Oil in Saginaw Michigan and Downeast Energy in Springvale, Maine, shared similar terminology, arguing that "massive positions held by speculators have contributed to price volatility that is simply unrelated to supply and demand fundamentals" (March 8, 2011and March 10, 2011, archived in Posch et al., 2019, Control Number IDs 31154 and 31536). Other small business owners invoked their own day-to-day expenses from "gas and food prices," urging the CFTC to use "strong position limits to control excessive gambling" by "banks, hedge funds or other financial players" (Handy Mickey Handyman Services, March 25, 2011, archived in Posch et al., 2019, Control Number ID 32565).

The use of moral language was, however, not limited to impassioned protests against the finance sector. Commenters from several parts of the agriculture sector assessed the moral aspect of regulatory decisions in concrete terms of market predictability and performance. Rather than falling into simple pro-and anti-regulation camps, these commenters drew distinctions among types of market participants. Some of the most sophisticated arguments came from producers, merchandisers, and retail users of grain. The ABA attributed excessive volatility in wheat prices to the arrival of financial investors such as index funds and argued against any increase in the allowable size of positions that such actors could take in wheat futures. Unlike the "traditional speculator" who acts as "medium between agricultural producers and end users through buying and selling," the "index speculators" buy and then "hold contracts by simply rolling their long-only positions to the next spot month." The bakers therefore worried that any increase in position limits available to index funds would increase the cost of hedging for the "country elevator, small flour miller or regional baker." Instead, the ABA "strongly opposed increasing speculative limits" and urged the CFTC to remove "the hedge exemption status from all index funds operating with the wheat futures markets" (March 28, 2011, archived in Posch et al., 2019, Control Number ID 33844).

In a subsequent round of comments on position limits, the large feed grain merchandisers elaborated on the arguments put forth by the bakers. The grain merchandisers used futures and options not only to hedge their own purchases, but also to facilitate risk management by their customers, the cattle producers. The Texas Cattle Feeders Association, for example, provided detailed criteria and distinctions with which the CFTC could write regulations to "prevent market manipulation, squeezes, and corners" without making it "more difficult for commercial end-users to hedge legitimate price risks arising from the normal course of business operations." Ultimately, the difference between unwarranted speculation and legitimate hedging was hard to define in the abstract. As a default approach, the cattle feeders suggested that the CFTC adopt a more refined definition of "bona fide hedging transactions" aimed at transactions in a "physical marketing channel" used by a "commercial enterprise" (February 10, 2014, archived in Posch et al., 2019, Control Number ID 59680).

The American Farm Bureau Association (AFBA), one of the sector's overarching trade groups, offered further clarification on the knotty issue of defining a bona fide hedging transaction by emphasizing the differences among specific markets and types of market participants. The AFBA questioned the CFTC's effort to define bona fide hedging by "enumerating specific types of transactions and excluding others." Such a "one size fits all" approach was "unlikely to provide the right solution for commodities as diverse as energy, metals, financial products and agricultural commodities" (February 10, 2014, archived in Posch et al., 2019, Control Number ID 59730).

A more substantive alternative to the CFTC's transaction-based approach came from another large trade group, the National Grain and Feed Association (NGFA), which echoed many of the AFBA's concerns and fleshed out others. A "well-crafted position limit regime" would, it suggested, need to rely upon "the expertise of traditional market participants and exchanges." This letter used language identical to the AFBA in applauding the regional exchanges (particularly the Chicago Board of Trade and the Kansas City Board of Trade) for facilitating consistent convergence between grain futures and physical-delivery spot markets-a process viewed as critical for price discovery and predictability in the commodity markets. The NGFA added that these exchanges had done extensive research on "the delicate balance between longs and shorts in each particular delivery market" and further asserted that this kind of "localized history is essential to make sure these markets converge properly to the benefit of the hedging customers." Much like the bakers, the NGFA asserted its belief "that nontraditional participants (i.e., investment capital) that have entered agricultural futures markets" had "contributed to contract performance problems" and hindered the consistent price convergence on which customary market participants depended (February 10, 2014, archived in Posch et al., 2019, Control Number ID 59681). Ultimately, on this view, the definition of a bona fide hedging transaction seemed to depend more on the identity of the hedger than the type of transaction used in effecting it.

While they did not only draw upon moral language, these organizations consistently placed their technical arguments within a larger moral frame of reference. By combining market expertise with a moral critique of purely speculative or manipulative behavior, these groups generated a rhetoric of fairness and market predictability aimed at the public interest. This was a language that firms in the financial services consistently avoided. By examining the entire set of public comments submitted during the key years for implementing the Dodd-Frank Act, our database illuminates the corpus as a single conversation composed of two distinct idioms.

Nonmarket commenters and-even more notably the nonfinancial firms-were especially concerned with food and household necessities (XIV). The different topics (X, XII, XIII, and XIV) that gather evaluative terms like speculation and manipulation delineate a cleavage that appears with increasing clarity toward the lower end of our commenter typology on topics that address the moral dimension of unrestrained derivatives trading.

Variations in composition among the four topics are important because they show that the contrast between technical and moral language cuts across the business versus nonbusiness divide. Market actors in agriculture, energy supply, and retail goods, often from rural states, conspicuously prioritize morally freighted language in many of their comments. At the same time, while many of their comment letters address the CFTC's proposed rules on position limits, they used similar language in comments on the end-user exemption and the definition of allowable or "bona fide" hedging that would be exempt from CFTC rules for documenting and clearing swap transactions.

To illuminate the dominant place of such moral claims in one of the two major parts of our corpus, it is well worth looking further into their substance. The use of moral language was far from simple or univocal. Different groups put moral argumentation to quite distinct uses in their comments on the Dodd-Frank rules.

Simple indignation was the primary theme in several waves of comment letters prompted by advocacy organizations such as AFR and the U.S. Public Interest Research Group. One such set, from small business owners of varied types, asked the CFTC to withhold end-user exemptions from purely financial organizations that "want to gamble in derivatives markets" in ways that "would throw important trades back into the shadows" (e.g., Rushland Landscapers, February 18, 2011, archived in Posch et al., 2019, Control Number ID 35253). Numerous oil and gas retailers such as Paxson Oil in Saginaw Michigan and Downeast Energy in Springvale, Maine, shared similar terminology, arguing that "massive positions held by speculators have contributed to price volatility that is simply unrelated to supply and demand fundamentals" (March 8, 2011and March 10, 2011, archived in Posch et al., 2019, Control Number IDs 31154 and 31536). Other small business owners invoked their own day-to-day expenses from "gas and food prices," urging the CFTC to use "strong position limits to control excessive gambling" by "banks, hedge funds or other financial players" (Handy Mickey Handyman Services, March 25, 2011, archived in Posch et al., 2019, Control Number ID 32565).

The use of moral language was, however, not limited to impassioned protests against the finance sector. Commenters from several parts of the agriculture sector assessed the moral aspect of regulatory decisions in concrete terms of market predictability and performance. Rather than falling into simple pro-and anti-regulation camps, these commenters drew distinctions among types of market participants. Some of the most sophisticated arguments came from producers, merchandisers, and retail users of grain. The ABA attributed excessive volatility in wheat prices to the arrival of financial investors such as index funds and argued against any increase in the allowable size of positions that such actors could take in wheat futures. Unlike the "traditional speculator" who acts as "medium between agricultural producers and end users through buying and selling," the "index speculators" buy and then "hold contracts by simply rolling their long-only positions to the next spot month." The bakers therefore worried that any increase in position limits available to index funds would increase the cost of hedging for the "country elevator, small flour miller or regional baker." Instead, the ABA "strongly opposed increasing speculative limits" and urged the CFTC to remove "the hedge exemption status from all index funds operating with the wheat futures markets" (March 28, 2011, archived in Posch et al., 2019, Control Number ID 33844).

In a subsequent round of comments on position limits, the large feed grain merchandisers elaborated on the arguments put forth by the bakers. The grain merchandisers used futures and options not only to hedge their own purchases, but also to facilitate risk management by their customers, the cattle producers. The Texas Cattle Feeders Association, for example, provided detailed criteria and distinctions with which the CFTC could write regulations to "prevent market manipulation, squeezes, and corners" without making it "more difficult for commercial end-users to hedge legitimate price risks arising from the normal course of business operations." Ultimately, the difference between unwarranted speculation and legitimate hedging was hard to define in the abstract. As a default approach, the cattle feeders suggested that the CFTC adopt a more refined definition of "bona fide hedging transactions" aimed at transactions in a "physical marketing channel" used by a "commercial enterprise" (February 10, 2014, archived in Posch et al., 2019, Control Number ID 59680).

The American Farm Bureau Association (AFBA), one of the sector's overarching trade groups, offered further clarification on the knotty issue of defining a bona fide hedging transaction by emphasizing the differences among specific markets and types of market participants. The AFBA questioned the CFTC's effort to define bona fide hedging by "enumerating specific types of transactions and excluding others." Such a "one size fits all" approach was "unlikely to provide the right solution for commodities as diverse as energy, metals, financial products and agricultural commodities" (February 10, 2014, archived in Posch et al., 2019, Control Number ID 59730).

A more substantive alternative to the CFTC's transaction-based approach came from another large trade group, the National Grain and Feed Association (NGFA), which echoed many of the AFBA's concerns and fleshed out others. A "well-crafted position limit regime" would, it suggested, need to rely upon "the expertise of traditional market participants and exchanges." This letter used language identical to the AFBA in applauding the regional exchanges (particularly the Chicago Board of Trade and the Kansas City Board of Trade) for facilitating consistent convergence between grain futures and physical-delivery spot markets-a process viewed as critical for price discovery and predictability in the commodity markets. The NGFA added that these exchanges had done extensive research on "the delicate balance between longs and shorts in each particular delivery market" and further asserted that this kind of "localized history is essential to make sure these markets converge properly to the benefit of the hedging customers." Much like the bakers, the NGFA asserted its belief "that nontraditional participants (i.e., investment capital) that have entered agricultural futures markets" had "contributed to contract performance problems" and hindered the consistent price convergence on which customary market participants depended (February 10, 2014, archived in Posch et al., 2019, Control Number ID 59681). Ultimately, on this view, the definition of a bona fide hedging transaction seemed to depend more on the identity of the hedger than the type of transaction used in effecting it.

While they did not only draw upon moral language, these organizations consistently placed their technical arguments within a larger moral frame of reference. By combining market expertise with a moral critique of purely speculative or manipulative behavior, these groups generated a rhetoric of fairness and market predictability aimed at the public interest. This was a language that firms in the financial services consistently avoided. By examining the entire set of public comments submitted during the key years for implementing the Dodd-Frank Act, our database illuminates the corpus as a single conversation composed of two distinct idioms.
