sections.header_raw: Assessing alternative perspectives: The moral contestation perspective
sections.header_norm: assessing alternative perspectives the moral contestation perspective
paragraphs: The sharp opposition that emerges in our data between technical legitimation and moral justification of policy preferences reveals an important dimension in the politics of financial regulation. Although almost absent from the literature on regulation in recent decades, there is intense concern among important groups of commenters with the fairness and the moral consequences of the rules by which markets for derivatives are governed. But this concern for the integrity of market competition is not displayed by all actors. Our typology allows us to show with precision which commenters avoid matters of market fairness and which commenters focus heavily on those issues. Because our database gives us a comprehensive view of all comments submitted during the key years of Dodd-Frank's implementation, it also allows us to assess how well other patterns-including the patterns indicative of capture and financialization-shaped the post-enactment debate over reforming the derivatives markets.

The theory of economic capture claims that incumbent firms will shape the rulemaking process to protect market share by creating new barriers to entry. In its strictly economic or "Stiglerian" formulation, this hypothesis implies a conflict of interest between large, concentrated suppliers and their customers. Within our typology of commenter groups, this divergence should appear between the major dealer banks and other sell-side actors that supply derivatives (commenter groups 1-4) versus the key buy-side actors such as the asset managers (commenter groups 5-6) and the large firms represented by the U.S. Chamber of Commerce (7). Instead, our data show more evidence of shared alignment between the priorities of the major derivative dealers and their larger customers in the asset management sector. This pattern of shared priorities shows broad similarity to the "leveraged interests" that Pagliari and Young (2014) find in their analysis of many comments across multiple regulatory agencies. At the same time, our data clearly contradict the narrowly economic concept of regulatory capture that privileges the incumbent dealer banks at the expense of all other market participants.

Our database shows somewhat more compatibility with the financialization perspective, which implies that firms across the financial services and a growing number of other sectors will share similar goals. In its strongest form, this perspective leads us to expect broad similarity of topic proportions spreading from the core sell-side investment banks (1) through the entire business world to the nonfinancial firms (11). Instead of this complete business sector congruity, we find a break in alignments after the U.S. Chamber of Commerce ( 7). The commenting activity of the infrastructure firms (8), professional advisors (9), and nonfinancial firms (10, 11) shows limited alignment with the supply-side actors. While finance is clearly relevant to all business sectors, the rise of finance does not erase stark differences in the concerns of market participants versus market infrastructure suppliers, large versus small firms, or coastal financial-service firms versus heartland agricultural, livestock, and natural-resource firms. These cleavages challenge the stronger versions of financialization, which imply that all businesses dependent on finance will share the interests of the core financial services sector.

The pattern that emerges far more consistently from our data than either the classic capture perspective or the strong financialization perspective is the separation between conspicuously technical and morally laden language used by different commenter types. Our analysis shows quite precisely where this separation appears: between our commenter group 9 (Law Firms, Consultants, and Advisors) and commenter group 10 (Nonfinancial, Private-Sector Associations). This division is therefore far from a simple opposition between business versus nonbusiness groups, because the nonfinancial associations and firms are among the most important contributors to the morally laden part of the debate over implementing Dodd-Frank. The clear use of morally justificatory arguments among important categories of market participants as well as nonmarket observers provides strong evidence for a new dimension in the politics of regulation-which we call the moral contestation perspective.

Without the moral contestation perspective, it is impossible to make sense of the contours revealed by our mapping of commenter types across the topics that comprise the entire corpus of comments submitted to the CFTC. This conclusion stems from the strong asymmetry we observe in the way the two parts of our commenter typology make use of morally justificatory language. Not only does such language make up a key strand running through the comment letters submitted by organizations in commenter types 10 through 17. Equally important for our argument, moral claims are all but absent from the segment of our corpus generated by comments from the core investment banks (1) through the financial market infrastructure firms (8) and the related law firms and other consultants (9).

Moral justification is deployed as a mode of assessing proposed rules by only one side of the debate, while the other side carefully eschews moral issues and insists that regulations be assessed on technical criteria presented as devoid of value judgments of any kind. Moral contestation thus not only played a major role in shaping the debate; it also represented one of two distinct idioms that are deployed in very different proportions across the main groups of organizations that engaged in debate over how best to implement the Dodd-Frank statutes on derivatives.

sections.header_raw: Interpretation and conclusions: Between technical legitimation and moral justification
sections.header_norm: interpretation and conclusions between technical legitimation and moral justification
paragraphs: The two distinct idioms of technical neutrality and moral evaluation that make up the debate over regulating derivatives express contrasting perspectives on the foundations of market capitalism. The language of neutral, technical, typically quantitative analysis of risk, liquidity, and other market parameters are characteristic of the market-fundamentalist, or "neoliberal," beliefs that have shaped economic policy debates over the last four decades. When this understanding of the economy was thrown into question by the bankruptcy of Lehman Brothers in 2008, it was hardly surprising that the ensuing turmoil sparked outrage at the actors that benefited from the crisis. By assembling public comments in the subsequent years-when some observers expected the immediate anger at the crisis to dissipate (Coffee, 2012)-our database shows that the crisis led a broad range of actors to reexamine such basic questions as who should be permitted to compete in which markets and on what terms.

Owing to its breadth and persistence, the language of moral contestation in our data is, we argue, evidence for the same issues of moral economy that we see in other areas of the modern political economy. It represents a deeper reassessment and potential readjustment of the principles of economic governance as they take shape, in our study, in the derivatives markets-the very markets that brought complex, hyper-financialized capitalism to its apogee in the first few years of the 21st century.

Although largely unappreciated in the scholarly literature on financial regulation in recent decades, the clash between these two alternative idioms has been highlighted in a growing number of the most recent studies. Several analysts have shown how the technical language of finance reinforces a managerial approach to economic policymaking. Far from a natural outgrowth of politically neutral market dynamics, this language is one of several tools that privilege organized business interests in the U.S. political economy. It supplies part of the connective tissue for a network of "legal scholars, business lawyers, policymakers, and commentators" who, according to Rahman and Thelen (2021), share "a common cultural, sociological, and ideological orientation" (p. 100). These common orientations allow neoliberal moral assumptions to be subsumed beneath the language of technical neutrality.

The idiom of technical neutrality thereby contributes to a form of elite cultural capture (Kwak, 2014) that is more diffuse but no less potent than the sharply etched dynamics of economic capture that earlier literature emphasized. A limited number of law firms and attorneys play a key role in advising banks and financial associations on their regulatory strategies. Careful analysis shows that the commenting firms gain a high degree of legal sophistication (Libgober, 2020b) and can pay as much as $100,000 for a single comment letter (Libgober, 2020a, p. 642). Detailed studies show that the complexity of the legal language used in financial-market regulation was especially important in shielding the derivatives markets from broad public scrutiny (Pistor, 2019, esp. pp. 144-152;Wigan, 2009).

The alternative idiom of moral contestation in our database challenges the language of technical neutrality by asking who should be allowed to participate in which markets, with what purposes, and with what degree of deference for customary practices. While this language arose in direct reaction to the crisis of 2008, it is deeply anchored in the history of commodities trading. The immediate anger at the banks and hedge funds took highly public form in the 2010s (Culpepper & Lee, 2020), but the issues of market integrity and fair pricing of early derivatives such as futures and options takes us deep into the history of markets for physical commodities. A comparison of cotton exchanges in 19th-century America, for example, shows how the Chicago Board of Trade adopted classification rules that privileged some market participants and encouraged high-risk speculation and market corners while the New Orleans Cotton Exchange imposed a more demanding quality grading system that discouraged outside speculators (Pinzur, 2016, pp. 433, 447 esp.).

It is no accident that the language of moral contestation in our database surfaces especially prominently in response to CFTC proposals to regulate physical commodities. As Eric Helleiner (2018) points out, the CFTC's proposed reforms for markets in agricultural and mineral commodities prompted many of the strongest reactions to the Dodd-Frank Act. The rules for these markets were supervised in the United States by a dual oversight structure, in which the agriculture committees as well as the competent finance and banking committees shared power in the U.S. Congress. The political salience of rules for pricing in agriculture and minerals was, however, far more than an artifact of legislative organization. Struggles over the pricing and delivery of physical commodities had long been a central dynamic by which cash markets for food and household necessities brought age-old forms of capitalism into existence.

After significant periods of stability, changes in agricultural markets often signal deeper reassessments of the moral economy that underpins widely accepted social arrangements. The 21st-century divide between technical legitimation versus moral justification of the rules for derivatives recapitulates dynamics very similar to those found by historian E. P. Thompson in his study of the moral economy of bread riots in 18th-century England (Thompson, 1971). The context and actors in today's derivatives markets are of course different from-and likely unrecognizable to-their 18th-century predecessors. But the disruption of well-established forms of exchange caused by deep-pocketed newcomers with little interest in the delivery of essential products to local communities is central to both periods of change.

In E. P. Thompson's analysis of 18th-century agricultural markets, the protesters were England's rural poor and the newcomers were the "Regrators, Engrossers, Forestallers, Hawkers and Jobbers of Corns, Cattle, and other Marketable Goods," (quoted in Thompson, 1971, p. 95) who bought up or otherwise withheld household necessities from village markets until prices rose. These newcomers prompted a range of protest actions by village residents who defended the prior principles of local exchange with far more strategic intention, Thompson argues, than prior accounts of the rural mob's "bread riots" had acknowledged.

By contrasting what he subsequently called "a paternalist model of food marketing" to the "new political economy of the free market in grain," Thomson's article prompted multiple waves of debate (Thompson, [1993(Thompson, [ ] 2015, pp. 260-261), pp. 260-261). Rather than attributing greater moral standing to either model, Thompson pointed out that the original article documented an asymmetry in the prominence of moral justification for the two models of provision. The proponents of the traditional, paternalist model were "moralists first and economists second," whereas "questions as to the moral polity" did not appear "in the new market theory … unless as preamble and peroration" (Thompson, 1971, p. 90;quoted in Thompson, [1993quoted in Thompson, [ ] 2015, p. 268), p. 268). In this sense Thompson was, as he characterized his argument it, "discriminating between two different sets of assumptions, two differing discourses (Thompson, [1993(Thompson, [ ] 2015, p. 271), p. 271)."

In the 21st century, a similar clash in the underlying justifications for different approaches to market regulation took shape in the comments submitted to the CFTC. The traditional market participants who wanted vigorous rules to preserve their customary ways of doing business were the established merchandisers and regional exchanges that serviced grain and livestock producers. The newcomers were the Wall Street investment banks and other finance firms. If subject to poorly crafted rules, the new group would, according to the Kansas City Board of Trade, be permitted to "own or control up to 25% … of KCBT wheat," enabling them to "influence the price of the core contract (to the benefit of the financial contract positions) by withholding a significant quantity of deliverable supply from the market." This dynamic-characterized by the KCBT as "the 'tail wagging the dog'"-was exactly the problem that industry participants saw as obstructing the all-important convergence of financial options and the monthly price of physically deliverable product (August 17, 2011, archived in Posch et al., 2019, Control Number ID 48088).

While only a portion of our database focuses on position limits, the distinction between legitimate versus opportunistic actors was articulated by commenters across a broad range of topics. This kind of justificatory framing went beyond agricultural producers to energy retailers, transport companies, single proprietorships from landscape designers to small-town lawyers, academic observers, citizens advocacy groups, and other nonprofit organizations. Gauged by the likelihood that a particular letter would rely on the terms most central to our last three topics, over 30% of the comments in our corpus exemplify the moral contestation perspective. They either exclusively expressed moral claims or, if they discussed more technical market parameters for parts of their argument, framed these points within the new idiom that highlighted the moral dimension of market regulation for the larger society.

While it does not demonstrate the effect of the morally comments on final rules, our analysis has clear implications for further research on the notice-and-comment process. Further research could illuminate whether the notice-and-comment process included an underappreciated strand of moral critique in earlier cases of financial market reregulation, such as following the savings and loan crisis of the late 1980s or the Enron bankruptcy in 2001. It would also be valuable to know whether the opposition of technical versus moral language appeared in comments submitted to the other agencies involved in implementing the Dodd-Frank legislation or whether it appeared only in response to the high stakes attached to the rules being proposed for the derivatives business by the CFTC.

Because derivatives became the quintessential example of opaque and complex instruments that characterized 21st-century capitalism following the 2008 Global Financial Crisis, the public comments analyzed here provide particularly important evidence for the way firms and organizations understand the challenges posed by 21stcentury capitalism. Our data show that a broad group of commenters, by insisting on a moral justification for financial regulation, effectively expanded what E. E. Schattschneider called the scope of public conflict-a shift that Judge-Lord (2019) documents in environmental regulation and that Carpenter (2023) illuminates in a broad range of other political arenas. Our analysis also suggests, as other studies have shown (Callaghan, 2018;Pagliari, 2018;Ramanna, 2022), that diverging views in the public comment process give regulators greater room for maneuver in resisting industry preferences. Finally, our analysis illuminates a latent coalition that favors more restrictive regulation of financial markets. Some participants are described by advocacy groups like Americans for Financial Reform, whose members include other watchdog groups, regional community organizers, labor unions, small retailers, and some religious activists. In addition, our research shows that selected larger businesses such as airlines as well as livestock enterprises, rural energy cooperatives, and grain distributors are voicing perspectives that make them available for mobilization around regulatory reforms.

To be sure, the articulation of a coherent, morally informed alternative for thinking about financial regulation does not by itself overturn the decades-old tendency by which "the technically complex" nature of modern finance has "squeezed the possibility for meaningful public participation out of the rule-making process" (Ford, 2017, p. 140). If the highly specialized, often legal language favored by the financial-services industry remains potent, however, our database shows that it was broadly contested by a very different language that aimed at anchoring the rule-making process in a forthright discussion of its consequences for the larger society. In this sense, we agree with the many authors who see a need to move beyond a focus on capture in order to analyze the entire arena of politics that shapes regulatory outcomes.

Our database also illuminates the line past which the powerful process of financialization did not extend. There is no doubt that the key dealer banks used derivatives in persuading asset managers, many large manufacturing firms, as well as their servicing law firms and advisors to adopt similar positions on the implementation of the Dodd-Frank provisions. But for a large number of retail energy suppliers, agricultural producers, and regional exchanges, financialization was a process so threatening that they preferred the language of moral contestation that resembled the comments of the pro-regulation advocacy shops, other watchdog organizations, many nonprofits, and even some labor unions.

Together, these groups show that an unlikely collection of engaged actors are developing a distinctive language of moral evaluation to challenge the dominant market players in derivatives and to contest a purely technical legitimation of the rules for financial regulation. By incorporating the language of moral justification into their comments, these actors are also establishing-in some ways retrieving-the notion that regulation must clearly serve a larger public interest. In their different ways, the commenters who use this new idiom are asking financial-market regulators to do much more than maintain the efficiency or the stability of financial markets. They are insisting that financial markets need to be anchored in rules that serve the public's interest in a vibrant and dependable productive economy for existing households and communities.

All "Organization" metadata values were computer-determined to be unique including nonsubstantive variations (spelling and punctuation) meaning that there are fewer than 4671 substantively unique organizations in the corpus. 4 That is, law firms which filed comments specifically on behalf of private asset managers were classified into private asset managers (5) rather than law firms, consultants, and related advisors (8).

In addition, our research shows that selected larger businesses such as airlines as well as livestock enterprises, rural energy cooperatives, and grain distributors are voicing perspectives that make them available for mobilization around regulatory reforms.

To be sure, the articulation of a coherent, morally informed alternative for thinking about financial regulation does not by itself overturn the decades-old tendency by which "the technically complex" nature of modern finance has "squeezed the possibility for meaningful public participation out of the rule-making process" (Ford, 2017, p. 140). If the highly specialized, often legal language favored by the financial-services industry remains potent, however, our database shows that it was broadly contested by a very different language that aimed at anchoring the rule-making process in a forthright discussion of its consequences for the larger society. In this sense, we agree with the many authors who see a need to move beyond a focus on capture in order to analyze the entire arena of politics that shapes regulatory outcomes.

Our database also illuminates the line past which the powerful process of financialization did not extend. There is no doubt that the key dealer banks used derivatives in persuading asset managers, many large manufacturing firms, as well as their servicing law firms and advisors to adopt similar positions on the implementation of the Dodd-Frank provisions. But for a large number of retail energy suppliers, agricultural producers, and regional exchanges, financialization was a process so threatening that they preferred the language of moral contestation that resembled the comments of the pro-regulation advocacy shops, other watchdog organizations, many nonprofits, and even some labor unions.

Together, these groups show that an unlikely collection of engaged actors are developing a distinctive language of moral evaluation to challenge the dominant market players in derivatives and to contest a purely technical legitimation of the rules for financial regulation. By incorporating the language of moral justification into their comments, these actors are also establishing-in some ways retrieving-the notion that regulation must clearly serve a larger public interest. In their different ways, the commenters who use this new idiom are asking financial-market regulators to do much more than maintain the efficiency or the stability of financial markets. They are insisting that financial markets need to be anchored in rules that serve the public's interest in a vibrant and dependable productive economy for existing households and communities.

sections.preview_head: The two distinct idioms of technical neutrality and moral evaluation that make up the debate over regulating derivatives express contrasting perspectives on the foundations of market capitalism. The language of neutral, technical, typically quantitative analysis of risk, liquidity, and other market parameters are characteristic of the market-fundamentalist, or "neoliberal," beliefs that have shaped economic policy debates over the last four decades. When this understanding of the economy was thrown into question by the bankruptcy of Lehman Brothers in 2008, it was hardly surprising that the ensuing turmoil sparked outrage at the actors that benefited from the crisis.
